data.title: UnitedHealth leads Humana, CVS as Medicare Advantage growth cools
data.description:
Americans enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans rose just over 3% from 2024 to 2025, marking one of the lowest growth rates for the program in years, healthcare policy organization KFF said this week, citing the latest federal enrollment data.

Among MA operators, UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) has further established its market leadership, while Humana (NYSE:HUM), the second-largest MA player, and CVS Health (NYSE:CVS) have both shed memberships, according to a report from Healthcare Dive.

Medicare Advantage (MA) plans had enrolled slightly over 34.4M Americans as of Feb. 1, indicating a 3.8% rise from a year ago and marking the program’s lowest growth rate in more than a decade, KFF said.

During the recently concluded open enrollment period, UnitedHealthcare, the insurance unit of UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), had expanded its MA memberships to 9.9M from 9.5M in December, while its rivals Humana (NYSE:HUM) and CVS Health’s (NYSE:CVS) Aetna division have both lost members.

HUM (NYSE:HUM), whose benefits ratio climbed to ~90% from ~87% amid a post-COVID spike in medical care among its enrollees, witnessed its membership base falling from 6.2M to 5.8M, roughly in line with the company’s target to shed approximately 550K MA members this year.

While MA memberships at CVS’ (NYSE:CVS) and Molina Healthcare (MOH) have dropped to ~4.2M and ~216.7K from ~4.4M and 244.7K, respectively, Centene’s (CNC) MA memberships held relatively steady at ~1.1M.

However, Elevance Health (ELV) and Cigna (CI) have expanded their MA memberships to ~2.3M and ~697.4K from ~2.0M and ~587.9K, respectively.

data.title: Key deals this week: Prosus, Equinox Gold, Saipem, Baidu and more
data.description:
Here's a list of key deals reported this week across sectors:

Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY) (OTCPK:PROSF) said on Monday that it plans to acquire food delivery major Just Eat Takeaway's (OTCPK:JTKWY) entire issued share capital for €20.30 per share via an all-cash public offer on the Amsterdam exchange for €4.1 billion ($4.3 billion).

Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) to acquire all the issued and outstanding common shares of Calibre Mining (OTCQX:CXBMF) in a court-approved plan of arrangement, the companies said on Sunday.

Saipem (OTCPK:SAPMF) (OTCPK:SAPMY) said Sunday it agreed to merge with rival oilfield services company Subsea7 (OTCPK:SUBCY) (OTCPK:ACGYF) in an all-stock deal valued at ~$4.63B.

Private equity firm Roark Capital is in advanced discussion to purchase Dave's Hot Chicken for about $1 billion.

CleanCore Solutions (NYSE:ZONE) on Tuesday said it has signed an agreement to acquire Sanzonate Europe, a distributor of aqueous ozone cleaning technologies in Europe.

Baidu (NASDAQ:BIDU) said it has entered into an agreement with JOYY to acquire the video-based entertainment live-streaming business in mainland China.

Dye & Durham (OTCPK:DYNDF) soared 35% after a report that a large shareholder made an offer to purchase the company for C$20 a share in cash.

Marqeta (NASDAQ:MQ) and TransactPay have entered into an agreement under which Marqeta will acquire TransactPay.

data.title: Top-rated biotechs with negative enterprise values
data.description:
After four consecutive years of underperformance, biotech stocks are among the most unloved segments in the market. Major industry benchmarks SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (NYSEARCA:XBI) and the iShares Biotechnology ETF (NASDAQ:IBB) have both trailed the S&P 500 (SP500) over the past 12 months.

Operating in an industry highly sensitive to interest rates, many biotechs are trading with negative enterprise values, meaning their net assets exceed their market capitalization.

If an investor buys all of the shares in a biotech with a negative EV and pays off its debts, cash and cash equivalents remaining in its balance sheet could be worth more than the investor’s outlay.

According to Seeking Alpha data, out of more than 500 publicly traded biotechs, about 120 biotechs are trading with negative enterprise values, meaning their value is more when dead than alive.

However, there are pockets of interest. According to Seeking Alpha Stock Screener, Wall Street has Strong Buy ratings on these 14 biotechs despite their negative EVs (market cap >$100M).

  1. Acelyrin (SLRN)
  2. Sutro Biopharma (STRO)
  3. iTeos Therapeutics (ITOS)
  4. Zenas BioPharma (ZBIO)
  5. Voyager Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VYGR)
  6. Perspective Therapeutics (NYSE:CATX)
  7. Nkarta (NKTX)
  8. TScan Therapeutics (TCRX)
  9. Artiva Biotherapeutics (ARTV)
  10. Annexon (ANNX),
  11. Erasca (ERAS)
  12. Acrivon Therapeutics (ACRV)
  13. Immatics (IMTX)
  14. Larimar Therapeutics (LRMR)

Including the stocks representing XBI and IBB, the following biotechs with negative EVs also join the top-rated list

  1. Alumis (ALMS)
  2. Climb Bio (CLYM)
  3. Lexeo Therapeutics (LXEO)
  4. Inozyme Pharma (INZY)
  5. Pyxis Oncology (PYXS)
  6. Mersana Therapeutics (MRSN)

data.title: Over 60% of companies post EPS beats this week-Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Insider trades: Coca Cola, Walmart among notable names this weekWall Street opened mixed on Friday after a key inflation reading came in-line with expectations. Markets were trying to recover from the sharp sell-off on Thursday after sentiment took a hit from a post-earnings fall in semiconductor giant Nvidia (NVDA) and more tariff-related moves by U.S. President Donald Trump.

Nvidia (NVDA) was down 8.5% at Thursday close even after it beat fiscal fourth-quarter results and offered up better-than-expected guidance, aided in part by its Blackwell line of GPUs. Analysts noted that the AI-behemoth’s results were good, but not good enough to justify its bubble-like valuation.

To add to investors’ worries, Trump confirmed that proposed 25% tariffs on Mexican and Canadian imports will go forward on March 4. He also said he would implement extra 10% tariffs on Chinese goods.

"Markets were in a stressed mood of their own, with renewed tariff threats from Trump in addition to a sharp tech sell-off that saw the Magnificent 7 post its worst day of 2025," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

Earnings roundup:

Looking at the earnings scorecard for the week, about 64% of the companies that reported earnings this week, beat EPS estimates. From the 53 names that reported earnings this week, 34 of them beat bottom-line estimates, while 29 of them topped revenue expectations.

Sector-wise performance:

Utilities: Among the companies from the Utilities sector that reported this week, some notable names included Vistra (VST) which saw an over 12% decline in its shares after its results, becoming one of the day’s largest losers on the S&P 500.

The company saw a plunge in its shares even after its Q4 adjusted EBITDA beat analyst expectations and more than doubled to $1.99B from $965M in the year-earlier quarter, driven by higher income across most segments.

Sempra (SRE) shares also saw a large decline after it reported weaker than expected Q4 adjusted earnings and revenues, and lowered its full-year guidance.

Technology: Among the tech names that reported earnings this week, Nvidia (NVDA) stole the spotlight, as it posted fiscal fourth-quarter results and offered up guidance that were better-than-expected.

For the period ending Jan. 26, the Jensen Huang-led company earned $0.89 per share on an adjusted basis. At the same time, revenue surged 78.8% year-over-year to come in at $39.33B.

Despite a beat on both top and bottom-line figures, shares of the semiconductor giant dropped after its earnings. Analysts noted that the results lacked the positive shock value of some of last year's reports.

Other prominent tech names that reported this week include HP (HPQ), and Salesforce (CRM), among others. For the quarter, HP reported adjusted earnings per share of $0.74, which matched the consensus estimate of $0.74. Revenue for the quarter totaled $13.5B, which was more than the estimate of $13.35B.

During the first quarter, HP's Personal Systems segment increased 5% year over year to total $9.2B. However, its Printing segment declined 2% year over year to $4.3B.

Real Estate: American Tower Corporation (AMT) Q4 earnings missed the average analyst estimate as operating expenses rose from the prior quarter, exceeding the Wall Street consensus. Guidance for 2025 AFFO per share also disappointed.

Meanwhile, Realty Income Corporation (O) reported 2025 guidance that was lower than average analyst estimate and Q4 earnings which were in line with the consensus estimate.

Healthcare: Agilent Technologies (A) reported results that beat on both lines. Its Q1 Non-GAAP EPS came at $1.31, on revenue of $1.68B. However, shares dipped in after-hours trading due to a disappointing fiscal year 2025 revenue guidance.

Viatris (VTRS) fell short of expectations on both top and bottom-line figures. Its Non-GAAP EPS of $0.54 missed estimates by $0.03, while revenue of $3.52B missed expectations by $80M.

Energy: Among the energy sector companies, while ONEOK (OKE) posted beats on both lines, EOG Resources (EOG) fell short of revenue estimates.

Consumer: Home Depot (HD) reported revenue rose 14.1% year-over-year to $39.7 billion in Q4. Comparable sales in the U.S. increased 1.3% for the quarter that ended on February 2 to beat the consensus expectation for a drop of 1.0%.

During a media interview, CFO Richard McPhail warned that he doesn't see the housing market improving in 2025.

Lowe's Companies (LOW) reported total sales in Q4 trickled 0.3% lower year-over-year to $18.6 billion. Comparable sales increased 0.2% during the quarter.

Meanwhile Domino's Pizza (DPZ) fourth quarter results were below Wall Street’s expectations, and declining traffic in its U.S. restaurants weighed on investor sentiment.

Communication Services: Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) reported a net loss of $494M for the three months ended December 31, compared to a net loss of $400M in the same period last year. The company recorded about $477M of impairment and restructuring charges in Q4.

On a per-share basis, it lost 20 cents, while analysts were expecting the New York-based company to report a profit of 3 cents.

Earnings next week:

For the upcoming week, about 10 companies are scheduled to report earnings, with AutoZone (AZO), Best Buy (BBY), and Ross Stores (ROST) reporting on Tuesday.

Wednesday will include names such as Brown-Forman (BF), and Campbell Soup (CPB), while Costco (COST), Broadcom (AVGO), and Hewlett Packard (HPE) will report on Thursday.

data.title: Vietnam PM calls for quick licensing for Musk's Starlink, trade rebalance with US
data.description:
Vietnam's Prime Minister Pham Minh Chinh on Saturday asked officias to rapidly issue a licence for Elon Musk’s Starlink internet satellite service.
The Prime Minister called on the Ministry of Science and Technology to approve a pilot license for Starlink, according to a post on the government's website.
In February, Vietnam approved rules that would allow Starlink to provide satellite internet services in the country while maintaining full ownership of the local subsidiary.
Chinh, speaking with a group of U.S. businessmen in Hanoi, said the Southeast Asian country was looking to rebalance its trade with the United States by purchasing products such as liquified natural gas, agriculture goods, pharmaceutical products and aircraft.
Chinh highlighted national carrier Vietnam Airlines' deal with Boeing (BA) in 2023 for the purchase of 50 737 Max planes, saying that the deal was worth $11 billion.
The Prime Ministers said he had asked Boeing (BA) to consider lowering the prices, noting budget carrier VietJet Aviation's order for 200 737 MAX jets.
Vietnam's trade surplus with the U.S. has widened amid booming exports, making its vulnerable to President Donald Trump's reciprocal tariffs.

data.title: Wall Street falls in February as tariff uncertainties, economic jitters hit sentiment
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) on Friday slipped 1.43% for February to end at 5,954.43 points. Its accompanying SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) fell 1.27% for the month.

February lived up to its reputation of being a traditionally weak month for markets. The month saw the benchmark S&P (SP500) notch an all-time intraday high of 6,147.43 points on February 19, and close at a record level of 6,144.15 on that same day. The index ended 3.09% below that record close on Friday.

Ryan Detrick, chief market strategist at Carson Group, pointed out that a positive January followed by a negative February was a pretty common occurrence. He highlighted that such a development was usually followed by a weak March, but also saw the final 10 months of the year doing a tad better than the average year. See the table he provided below:

Tariffs dominated headlines in February. The enthusiasm sparked by Donald Trump's election victory last year and his inauguration last month has waned due to uncertainties sparked by the President's trade and tariff moves.

Moreover, data has pointed to stubborn inflation and a slowdown in the economy, leading to further jitters. The indicators have been enough to spur the Federal Reserve into sending a clear message that it was ready to hold interest rates steady.

"We end the week where we stood one month ago with the looming threat of significant U.S. tariff hikes. At that time, we emphasized that a sustained 25% tariff hike imposed on U.S. imports from Canada and Mexico would generate a large and disruptive negative supply shock. Furthermore, we argued that 'we should not underestimate the resilience of the U.S. expansion to weather modest shocks, or the power of large shocks to shake the foundations of U.S. exceptionalism,'" JPMorgan's Bruce Kasman said on Friday.

"One month on, our confidence in the underlying resilience of the U.S. expansion has not wavered, despite incoming reports pointing to soft current-quarter GDP growth. However, we are becoming more concerned that U.S. policy actions will weigh on demand and damage sentiment in a manner that shakes the foundation of the expansion," Kasman added.

The analyst was referring to the fact that the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow model on Friday updated its growth expectations for U.S. Q1 2025 real GDP to -1.5%, from +2.3% earlier.

Turning to the monthly performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) sectors, investors shunned risky growth stocks. Consumer Discretionary was the top loser, sliding more than 9%, followed by Communication Services with a more than 6% drop. Still, six sectors in total gained for the month, led by defensive stalwart Consumer Staples.

See below a breakdown of the performance of the sectors as well as their accompanying SPDR Select Sector ETFs from January 31 close to February 28 close:

#1: Consumer Staples +5.59%, and the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) +5.19%.

#2: Real Estate +4.10%, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) +4.18%.

#3: Energy +3.28%, and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) +3.83%.

#4: Health Care +1.37%, and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV) +1.40%.

#5: Financials +1.28%, and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLF) +1.38%.

#6: Utilities +1.17%, and the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) +1.23%.

#7: Materials -0.15%, and the Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLB) -0.03%.

#8: Information Technology -1.41%, and the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLK) -2.29%.

#9: Industrials -1.60%, and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLI) -1.46%.

#10: Communication Services -6.30%, and the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC) -0.37%.

#11: Consumer Discretionary -9.42%, and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) -6.98%.

For investors looking to track the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500), here are some exchange-traded funds of interest: (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

For investors looking into the future of what's happening, take a look at the Seeking Alpha Catalyst Watch to see next week's breakdown of actionable events that stand out.

data.title: China telling AI executives avoid travel to US over security concerns: report
data.description:
Beijing is telling artificial intelligence researchers and entrepreneurs to avoid visits to the U.S., The Wall Street Journal reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The Journal said officials are worried that Chinese AI experts traveling could disclose confidential information on the country’s progress with AI.

The Journal quoted people in the tech issue as saying there is no outright travel ban but there is guidance from Chinese officials in Beijing, Shanghai, and the province of Zhejiang. Zhejiang is home to Alibaba (BABA) and DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK).

The people said officials there are discouraging local AI executives and those in other sensitive industries to not take nonurgent trips to the US and its allies, The Journal said. Executives who do travel are told to disclose their plans before departing and to brief officials on their activities abroad when they return, the Journal added.

DeepSeek founder Liang Wenfeng declined an invitation to last month’s AI summit in Paris, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter.

Some of the people reportedly said another AI startup’s founder cancelled a planned trip to the U.S. after instructions from the Chinese government, according to the report.

China’s embassy in Washington, D.C. did not immediately respond to a request for comment by Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Trending stocks as market closes volatile final week of February
data.description:
Wall Street faced off rising geopolitical tensions, trade war signals and economic slowdown in a wild week that capped off February.

The S&P 500 ended the week 0.75% lower and closed the month around 0.7% down. The Dow Jones Industrial Average had a more positive week, closing 1.4% higher, while the Nasdaq Composite was up 1.6%.

Analysts and traders have projected volatile waters ahead for the market after U.S. President Donald Trump got into a shouting match with his Ukrainian counterpart, Volodymyr Zelenskiy.

Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent welcomed Mexico’s proposal to match U.S. tariffs on China and urged the same from Canada. A slew of tariffs from both countries could signal more unpredictability ahead.

Consumer spending appeared soft as well, with January core personal consumption expenditures price index rising 2.6% Y/Y and inflation-adjusted consumer spending down 0.5%, the biggest monthly decline in nearly four years.

Amid these shocks in the market, these were some of the trending stocks:

Chinese electric vehicle stocks advanced over the week despite concerns that a trade war between the U.S. and China could darken the sector. XPeng (XPEV) led the pack with +18.8% gain, while Li Auto (LI) rose +16.2%.

Super Micro Computer (SMCI) came in spotlight as the company filed its "delinquent" financial reports with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission just ahead of its deadline. Shares initially jumped on the news, but declined 23% for the week as investors reacted negatively to the 10-K filing.

B Riley Financial (RILY) soared 73% as the company regained compliance with Nasdaq listing rule.

Cryptocurrency-exposed stocks slide as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) tumbled below $85,000, paring the gains seen since Donald Trump’s election to the White House. MicroStrategy (MSTR) was down -23% for the week, CleanSpark (CLSK) fell 13.7%, Hut 8 (HUT) -10.9%, Coinbase Global (COIN) -9.7%, Galaxy Digital Holdings (OTCPK:BRPHF) -4.6%, Bakkt Holdings (BKKT) -12%, Riot Platforms (RIOT) -12.4%, MARA Holdings (MARA) -4.6%, HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE) -17% and Bit Digital (BTBT) dropped -8.5%.

Eli Lilly (LLY) was in focus as the drugmaker expanded its weight loss drug offerings, launching new 7.5 mg and 10 mg single-dose vial formats of Zepbound (tirzepatide) at $499 through its direct-to-consumer platform, LillyDirect, nearly 23% cheaper than its injector pen versions.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) shares tumbled after-hours on Monday after the telehealth company reported fourth-quarter financial results and said it will soon stop selling some compound weight-loss drugs.

SoundHound (SOUN) advanced 8.4% after its fourth quarter results beat estimates. The company also raised its full-year 2025 revenue outlook to a range of $157M-$177M (midpoint: $167M), up from the prior $155M-$175M range and above the $165.27M consensus.

Applovin (APP) fell 20.9% after Fuzzy Panda and Culper Research each released short reports against the company.

NetApp (NTAP) dropped 19.7% after the tech firm released its third quarter fiscal 2025 financial results, with revenue falling below expectations.

Rocket Lab (RKLB) shares fell 18% at market open on Friday after report the company reported fourth quarter earnings and issued an Q1 sales outlook that fell below expectations.

Tesla (TSLA) erased 13% after data from the European Automobile Manufacturers Association showed that the EV maker’s sales in Europe dropped 45% in January. Shares of the Elon Musk-led company are down about 40% from their late 2024 high.

data.title: Real estate stocks stand out as winners in February despite a rise in bear sentiment
data.description:
S&P 500 real estate stocks closed February ~5% higher on average, while the major market averages ended their journey in red amid a surge in bearish sentiment.

Bearish sentiment among individual levels surged to the highest level in 2-1/2 years this past week as the tech and momentum trades showed some vulnerability.

The benchmark S&P 500 concluded February down by 1.4%, driven by uncertainties stemming from President Donald Trump's tariff moves and hot inflation data.

Investors are selling off risk assets, with the risk appetite on a decline since Trump ramped up tariff threats for Canada, Mexico, China, and even the European Union.

The second month of the year has traditionally been a weak one for markets, but the defensive sectors and bond-sensitive stocks have recently performed better than other areas of the stock market.

"The rotation from equities to bonds continues apace, as evidenced by the drop in the 10-year yield. The 10-year is down from 4.6% at the inauguration to 4.2% today – we believe it has further to fall," Alex King, investing group leader of Cestrian Capital Research, told Seeking Alpha.

Real estate stocks have been on a gaining spree amid falling long-term yields and mortgage rates. The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) saw outflows worth $10.56M on Monday, but logged $32.17M of inflows on Wednesday.

For the last week of the month, the S&P 500 Real Estate Index Sector (SP500-60) rose 2.13% to 271.05, while the accompanying The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) added 2.49% to close at 43.15.

The Dow Jones Equity All REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) gained 1.92% on a weekly basis to finish at 2,730.07.

The FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs index (INDEXFTSE: FNER), which includes all tax-qualified REITs with more than 50% of assets in qualifying real estate other than mortgages secured by real property that also meet minimum size and liquidity criteria, advanced 2.18% W/W to 796.74.

Meanwhile, S&P 500 closed the week 0.98% lower at 5,954.43.

Seeking Alpha analysts continue to grade XLRE as Buy. But the Quant Rating system changed its stand on the ETF to Buy from Hold today, with a focus on Momentum.

Optimism

Real estate brokerage leaders reported record-high optimism in profitability, transactions, and market growth, the latest Delta Real Estate Leadership Survey said.

"After several years of uncertainty, brokerages are navigating market changes with renewed optimism and strategic focus. They see opportunities in technology, talent acquisition, and overall market share growth. Despite the challenges 2025 will present, the brokerage leaders we surveyed are bullish on success," said Michael Minard, CEO of Delta Media Group.

Majority of the real estate investors are planning to grow their portfolios and invest significantly in property improvements in 2025, another survey from property management software provider RentRedi showed.

2025 housing market will be slightly better than 2024 in terms of originations and sales, said Ryan McKeveny, the managing director of mortgage and real estate at Zelman & Associates, at the Housing Economic Summit by the housing market and industry data information service provider, HousingWire.

Company News

  • Matterport (MTTR) and Costar (CSGP) said the HSR waiting period for their transaction has expired, though the companies are still working with the Federal Trade Commission. The companies continue to work "cooperatively" with the FTC in its review of the transaction and expect that the deal will be completed in the Q1.
  • Invitation Homes (INVH) reported Q4 FFO of $0.47 and revenue of $659.13M (+5.6% Y/Y), in-line with consensus. Separately, INVH announced the promotion of Timothy Lobner to executive vice president and COO post, effective March 2.
  • Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) acquired Del Monte Shopping Center for $123.5M.
  • Extra Space Storage (EXR) delivered Q4 earnings and revenue that exceeded the Wall Street consensus. Meanwhile, the company issued 2025 guidance that assumes a slowly improving operating environment.
  • Simon Property Group (SPG), Shopify and Leap commerce platform for physical retail are collaborating to help ecommerce brands open brick-and-mortar locations across Simon's portfolio of properties.
  • American Tower (AMT) Q4 earnings missed the average analyst estimate as operating expenses rose from the prior quarter, exceeding the Wall Street consensus. Guidance for 2025 AFFO per share also disappointed.
  • Public Storage (PSA) issued 2025 guidance incorporated the estimated impact from the Los Angeles wildfires, following Q4 earnings that missed the average analyst estimate.
  • SBA Communications (SBAC) declared $1.11/share quarterly dividend, 13.3% increase from the prior dividend of $0.98. Separately, SBAC posted Q4 FFO of $3.47, which beats by $0.19, and revenue of $693.7M (+2.8% Y/Y), which beats by $11.3M.
  • Realty Income (O) delivered 2025 guidance that was lower than the average analyst estimate and posted Q4 earnings in-line with the consensus estimate.

Winner & Losers

Residential REITs stood out as the winner among the S&P 500 REIT subsectors this week, adding 4.55% from last week on average. Most of the gains were driven by Invitation Homes (INVH) (+6.65% W/W) and Essex Property Trust (ESS) (+5.71% W/W)

Health Care REITs followed, advancing 3.99% on a weekly basis. Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) was the second best performer for the week among the individual S&P 500 real estate stocks (+7.00% W/W).

American Tower (AMT) led the pack with a 7.35% increase from last week, despite an earnings miss as the telecom tower REIT shifts focus to data centers.

Hotel & Resort REITs was the only REIT subsector to end in red, with Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) recording 1.29% of weekly losses. Digital Realty Trust (DLR) (-5.47% W/W) and Extra Space Storage (EXR) (-2.17% W/W) topped the list of S&P 500 real estate losers.

The S&P 500 Real Estate Management & Development index bounced back to green after three consecutive weeks of losses, advancing 2.26% as mortgage rates hit a two-month low.

For the broader real estate sector, Avalon GloboCare (ALBT) (+33.76% W/W), Medical Properties Trust (MPW) (+21.15% W/W), and Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) (+14.11% W/W) were the top gainers of the week.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: AppLovin selloff follows significant insider sales
data.description:
Shares of AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) plunged ~22% this week, extending an ~18% drop in the prior week after two short seller reports from Fuzzy Panda Research and Culper Research alleged fraudulent activity related to the company’s AXON advertising software.

AppLovin’s (NASDAQ:APP) recent selloff contrasts its meteoric rise in 2024 when shares of the Palo Alto, California-based firm surged over 700% even as the company insiders locked in much of those gains through some of the notable sales last year.

However, according to data from OpenInsider, no APP insiders bought company shares in 2024, which, according to Seeking Alpha analyst Hataf Capital, represented a major bearish signal. “The fact that no insider purchases were recorded during the whole year of intense selling activity further reinforces the bearish signal,” Hataf Capital wrote.

The analyst pointed to more than $1.6B share sale by KKR (KKR) in November when the early AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) investor fully divested its company stake. “This large-scale exit by a sophisticated institutional investor that likely has deep insight into the company's operations and prospects is particularly concerning,” the analyst argued at the time.

However, technology chief Vasily (Basil) Shikin’s $189.5M worth of share sale, which cut its ownership stake by ~11% just before KKR’s divestment, was also significant, Hataf Capital wrote.

“The timing and size of these sales from the company's technical leader, given AppLovin's heavy emphasis on its AI-driven AXON technology, raise questions about the sustainability of the company's competitive advantage,” the analyst added. Piling on to insider sales, co-founder and CEO Adam Foroughi sold more than $72M worth of APP stock across four transactions in 2024.

While Hataf Capital reasoned that significant insider selling can be common after a sharp stock price appreciation, the analyst cautioned that “the concentration and magnitude of these sales in November 2024 far exceed typical profit-taking behavior.”

However, both Foroughi and Shikin remain very much invested in the company, directly owning ~2.8M and ~3.5M APP shares, respectively, according to the latest SEC filings.
